ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Ayam Geprek. FOODON_03301116 (fried chicken food product) is used as the closest parent. The smashed preparation and sambal topping are not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00GJ (Chicken meat, fried) is the closest defensible parent. Ayam Geprek involves smashing the fried chicken and topping with sambal geprek; these preparation steps are not represented in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for Ayam Geprek or Indonesian smashed fried chicken.

serving.description: 1 serving (1 piece boneless chicken leg with sambal and side salad)
serving.natural_language_portion: One typical serving of Ayam Geprek consists of one boneless fried chicken leg piece smashed and topped with sambal geprek, accompanied by a small side salad, weighing approximately 250 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: Fried chicken (battered, from fast food / restaurant), per 100 g — adapted with sambal chili component using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy and FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service (FoodData Central SR Legacy) and FAO/INFOODS
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Ayam Geprek exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for battered deep-fried chicken leg (NDB 05298 / FDC ID 171477) with an adjustment for sambal geprek (chili-garlic paste, approximately 15 g per 100 g of dish). The composite estimate reflects a typical restaurant-style Ayam Geprek. Energy and macros are weighted accordingly. All values are per 100 g of the combined dish as served.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fried chicken and battered poultry products — analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken (GI ~45) from the University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Ayam Geprek in the University of Sydney GI Database. The GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on battered deep-fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values in the range of 40–50 for battered fried chicken products. The sambal geprek component (chili-garlic paste) contributes negligible carbohydrate and is not expected to materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (45 × 23.0) / 100 = 10.35, rounded to 10.4. Serving carbohydrate estimated at 23.0 g (9.2 g/100 g × 250 g serving).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~420 mg/100 g; ~1050 mg per serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Ayam Geprek to occasional consumption, as a single 250 g serving provides approximately 1050 mg sodium — nearly half the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — primarily from the seasoned batter, frying process, and sambal geprek.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~10.4 per serving) and high fat content from deep-frying
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Ayam Geprek in moderation; the low-to-moderate GI (approximately 45) and moderate glycemic load of ~10.4 per serving are manageable, but the high total fat from deep-frying (approximately 33.8 g per serving) may impair insulin sensitivity and should be factored into overall dietary fat targets.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat, saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium from deep-frying and seasoned batter
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat Ayam Geprek as an occasional food, as a 250 g serving delivers approximately 33.8 g total fat, 8.5 g saturated fat, 195 mg cholesterol, and 1050 mg sodium — all of which are clinically significant risk factors for atherosclerosis and hypertensive cardiovascular events.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ayam Geprek is a popular Indonesian fried chicken dish originating from Yogyakarta, Indonesia, and now widely consumed across Southeast Asia including Singapore. The dish consists of a boneless chicken piece — typically a leg or thigh — coated in a seasoned wheat flour batter and deep-fried until golden and crispy. The fried chicken is then placed on a stone mortar or flat surface and smashed (geprek means 'to smash' in Javanese) to crack the crust and allow the sambal geprek to penetrate the meat. The sambal geprek is a raw or lightly cooked chili paste made from red bird's eye chilies or devil's chilies, garlic, shallots, and salt, pounded together to a coarse, fiery condiment. The result is a dish with contrasting textures — a shattered crispy exterior and juicy, tender chicken interior — drenched in intensely spicy, garlicky sambal. Ayam Geprek is typically served with steamed white rice and a simple side salad of fresh vegetables. Nutritionally, the dish is a significant source of protein (~17.8 g/100 g) and fat (~13.5 g/100 g) due to deep-frying, with moderate carbohydrate from the batter (~9.2 g/100 g). Sodium content is high (~420 mg/100 g), making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 45 (low), with a moderate glycemic load per serving (~10.4), making it relatively manageable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled. The dish is halal and dairy-free but contains gluten from the wheat flour batter.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Ayam Geprek?
Is Ayam Geprek good for diabetics?

ingredient_synonyms.sambal geprek: smashed chili paste, raw chili sambal, devil's chili sambal

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Ayam Geprek exists in any approved primary regional source (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy battered deep-fried chicken data (FDC ID 171477) with a composite adjustment for the sambal geprek component. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on battered fried chicken entries. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (battered deep-fried chicken leg, FDC ID 171477) combined with a proportional adjustment for sambal geprek (~15 g per 100 g of dish). This is a composite estimate; exact values will vary by restaurant preparation, oil type, batter thickness, and sambal recipe. 2. Vitamin D is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source entry for this food category and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on battered fried chicken entries in the University of Sydney GI Database (GI range 40–50 for battered fried poultry); no direct GI measurement for Ayam Geprek was found. 4. The field dataed in the source input (Smile Martabak - Funan, field survey data) is noted for provenance tracing only; field survey is a forbidden source for nutrient data and was not used for any nutritional values. 5. Cuisine tags in the input JSON ('Stuffed Pancake', 'Pancake', 'Coffee') appear to be restaurant-level tags from Smile Martabak and are not relevant to Ayam Geprek as a dish; they have been disregarded. 6. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data and are approximate. 7. Vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food and no reliable estimate is available from approved sources.
